5.text: Click is imported instead of argparse because it provides a decorator-based API that's more readable for CLIs with many options. Each @click.option below maps directly to a CLI flag — the function signature is the interface definition.

6.text: sys is used for sys.stdin (reading piped input) and sys.exit() (returning proper exit codes). Exit code 1 signals failure to shell scripts that chain commands.

8.text: The core engine is imported at module level (unlike the SDK's lazy import) because the CLI module is only loaded when the user runs the command — there's no startup penalty to worry about.

9.text: Three specific exception types are imported for the error handling strategy below. Each AWS failure mode gets a distinct, user-friendly error message instead of a raw stack trace.

12.text: The @click.command decorator transforms this function into a CLI entry point. context_settings enables both -h and --help — a small UX detail that matches ttok's behavior.

13.text: @click.version_option() adds --version automatically. Click reads the version from the package metadata (set in pyproject.toml), so there's no hardcoded version string to keep in sync.

14.text: nargs=-1 means "accept any number of positional arguments." This is what lets users write ttok4bedrock hello world — the words are collected into a tuple and joined with spaces later.

15.text: The -i flag accepts a file path and Click automatically opens it for reading. This handles file-not-found errors with a clean message before the function body even runs.

17.text: The -t flag switches the tool from counting mode to truncation mode. This mirrors ttok's interface exactly — same flag letter, same semantics.

19.text: The default model ID is repeated here (same as in __init__.py). This duplication is intentional — the CLI and SDK are independent entry points that shouldn't depend on each other's defaults.

21.text: The --aws-region option is Bedrock-specific — ttok doesn't have it. This is the only CLI flag that breaks strict ttok compatibility, but it's necessary for multi-region AWS setups.

22.text: Click maps each decorator to a function parameter by name. The function signature becomes the CLI's contract — adding a new option is just another decorator + parameter.

66.text: Input priority: if no positional args and no file, fall back to stdin. This three-source input strategy (args > file > stdin) is what makes the tool composable in shell pipelines — you can pipe, redirect, or pass inline text.

67.text: Assign stdin as the input source. Click's sys.stdin integration means the tool works naturally in pipelines: cat file.txt | ttok4bedrock.

69.text: Positional arguments are joined with spaces to reconstruct the original text. ttok4bedrock hello world becomes "hello world".

71.text: When both a file and arguments are provided, the file content comes first. This matches ttok's behavior and lets users do things like ttok4bedrock -i header.txt "extra text".

72.text: Read the entire file into memory. For token counting, we need the full text — streaming wouldn't help since the API requires the complete input.

73.text: If both file and arguments are provided, concatenate them with a space. This edge case is handled for ttok compatibility.

78.text: Empty input is caught early with a clear error. Without this guard, the Bedrock API would receive an empty message and return a confusing error about invalid input format.

79.text: Error output goes to stderr (err=True) so it doesn't corrupt stdout when the tool is used in a pipeline. echo "text" | ttok4bedrock 2>/dev/null would suppress errors while keeping the count.

80.text: Exit code 1 signals failure. Shell scripts can check $? to detect errors: ttok4bedrock "text" || echo "failed".

84.text: The counter is created once and reused for both the count and potential truncation. Unlike the SDK functions (which create a new counter per call), the CLI benefits from the LRU cache across operations within a single invocation.

87.text: The mode branch: -t flag present → truncate and output text; absent → count and output number.

88.text: Truncation delegates to the core engine. The CLI passes the raw -t value as max_tokens — no validation needed since Click already ensures it's an integer.

90.text: nl=False is critical for pipeline correctness. If truncated text is piped to a file, an extra newline would change the token count of the output. The count path (line 89) uses the default nl=True because a trailing newline after a number is expected.

93.text: Token counting is the default mode — no flags needed. The result is a single integer printed to stdout, ready for shell variable capture: TOKENS=$(ttok4bedrock "text").

95.text: The count is printed with a trailing newline (Click's default). This matches the convention for CLI tools that output a single value — wc -l, du -s, etc.

97.text: The error handling cascade starts here. Each except block catches a specific AWS failure mode and translates it into a message that tells the user exactly what to do — not just what went wrong.

98.text: The error message tells users exactly how to fix the problem: aws configure or environment variables. Generic "authentication failed" messages leave users guessing.

103.text: sys.exit(1) after each error ensures the process terminates with a non-zero exit code. This is critical for CI/CD pipelines and shell scripts that check exit status.

105.text: NoRegionError is separate from NoCredentialsError because the fix is different. Credentials need aws configure; region needs --aws-region or AWS_DEFAULT_REGION. Specific errors → specific guidance.

113.text: ClientError is the catch-all for Bedrock API errors (invalid model, throttling, access denied). The error code and message are extracted from the boto3 response structure to give users actionable information.

115.text: Extracting the error code and message from the nested response dict. The .get() chain with defaults ensures this never crashes even if the error response has an unexpected structure.

117.text: Format the error as a single line with the error code in parentheses. This makes it easy to grep for specific error types in logs: ttok4bedrock "text" 2>&1 | grep ValidationException.

118.text: Exit with code 1 for API errors. All error paths use the same exit code — the error message on stderr distinguishes the failure type.

120.text: The final catch-all ensures the CLI never shows a raw Python traceback to users. Any unexpected error gets a clean one-line message and exit code 1.

126.text: The if __name__ guard allows running the CLI directly with python -m ttok4bedrock.cli during development, in addition to the ttok4bedrock entry point defined in pyproject.toml.
